risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group." Comment: Although the phrase "force equal sample sizes" reflects restricted allocation, the study explicitly says participants were "randomly assigned" and the use of restricted random assignment is consistent with a proper random sequence rather than a predictable rule. This is sufficient for Low risk under a permissive standard.

_evidence.method_quote: Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group.
_evidence.method_summary: Eligible families were randomly assigned to HNC or TE-HNC using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The study reports that "Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group," but it does not describe any method used to conceal the upcoming assignment from the person enrolling participants. Comment: This is insufficient to confirm a concealment mechanism such as central allocation, SNOSE, or identical numbered containers; therefore allocation concealment remains unclear.

_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation as “Using restricted random assignment to force equal sample sizes, Master’s-level therapists randomly assigned the remaining 22 eligible low-income families to the HNC or TE-HNC group,” but it does not describe how the upcoming assignment was concealed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "the project provided identical smartphone models to all participating families in order to ensure that the human-interface (i.e., design, usability, interactivity) of the smartphone enhancements were identical for all families" Comment: This indicates an effort to standardize the intervention interface across groups, reducing the likelihood that participants or delivering personnel could distinguish groups from the device itself. Although the study does not explicitly state that participants/personnel were fully blinded, the main outcomes included both subjective and objective measures, and the intervention was delivered by therapists in both arms with the same general HNC program; the described interface standardization supports low performance-bias risk overall for this study's dominant outcomes.

_evidence.blinding_quote: "Smartphone ownership was not an inclusion/exclusion criterion as the project provided identical smartphone models to all participating families in order to ensure that the human-interface (i.e., design, usability, interactivity) of the smartphone enhancements were identical for all families"

risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The primary outcome assessor is the patient/caregiver self-report, and the study explicitly states that the caregiver-report ECBI was used as the dependent measure: "Intensity and Problem subscales on the 36-item ECBI ... served as the dependent measure of behavior change." It also states that the HNC Consumer Satisfaction Scale "assesses caregiver satisfaction" and that daily skill practice was reported by caregivers. Comment: Because the assessors were the participants/caregivers themselves and these outcomes are subjective/patient-reported, lack of blinding could directly influence outcome measurement. The extracted evidence also indicates "participants_blinded": "unclear" and no assessor blinding quote is provided, but the patient-reported nature of the outcomes is sufficient to judge high risk under the worst-outcome rule.

Quote: "The four families (HNC = 2; TE-HNC = 2) who dropped out of the study notified project staff prior to discontinuing participation and each cited a major health (e.g., organ transplant) or family (e.g., divorce) stressor necessitating dropout." Comment: The analysed outcome had 4/22 randomized families lost overall (about 18%), balanced across arms (2 vs 2; 0 pp differential), with reasons unrelated to the outcome. The paper also states, "we considered only complete data (i.e., data from participants available at pre- and post-treatment)," so the complete-case analysis was based on the remaining participants rather than unreported missing outcome data. Although three randomized families were practice cases excluded from analyses, this was a design feature rather than post-randomization attrition for the outcome of interest.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “The four families (HNC = 2; TE-HNC = 2) who dropped out of the study notified project staff prior to discontinuing participation and each cited a major health (e.g., organ transplant) or family (e.g., divorce) stressor necessitating dropout.” Also, “The initial three families randomized served as practice cases for each of the three project therapists,” and “the remaining 19 families HNC (n = 10) and TE-HNC (n = 9) were considered for use in the current analyses.”

“The four families (HNC = 2; TE-HNC = 2) who dropped out of the study notified project staff prior to discontinuing participation and each cited a major health (e.g., organ transplant) or family (e.g., divorce) stressor necessitating dropout.” Also, “The initial three families randomized served as practice cases for each of the three project therapists,” and “the remaining 19 families HNC (n = 10) and TE-HNC (n = 9) were considered for use in the current analyses.”

_evidence.notes: Primary analyses used only families with complete pre- and post-assessment data (“we considered only complete data”). Three randomized families were practice cases and excluded from analyses; attrition was 4/22 among randomized families, but 7/19 TE-HNC and 8/19 HNC were analyzed among the remaining analytic sample.
